summary: The main claim is: The extension sends the content of each loaded page to OpenAI API, and asks GPT if the page should be blocked.
The main claim is: It relies on two key innovations: values cards, and the moral graph.
To prevent this, we need models that can act wisely on our values.
The main claim is: The smarter model will likely need to find a way to figure out the utility functions of the dumber models.
Every time the model does something that harms the utility function of the dumber models, it gets a loss function.

The main claim is: "I think it is going to be pro-humanity from the standpoint that humanity is just much more interesting than not-humanity." My personal comments: Sorry, but at face value, this just does not seem a great plan from safety perspective.
The main claim is: - Examples of discussions where the manipulation techniques are used effectively against humans.
Train AIs to not use manipulation techniques, and instead to use asymmetric persuasion techniques.
The main claim is: ^ The prompt was simply Please write a poem about petertodd.
To explore this, I used the prompt This is the tale of Leilan and petertodd .

The main claim is: Or rather, I think sufficiently capable AI systems should be more data efficient than humans (I expect that sufficiently advanced engineering would be more energy efficient than biology).
I think this suggests that cumulative returns to cognitive capabilities from increased computational resources (training data and compute) are strongly sublinear .

top_comment.author: Mitchell_Porter
top_comment.text:
The MIRI 2000s paradigm for an AI capable of self-improvement, was that it would be modular code with a hierarchical organization, that would potentially engage in self-improvement at every level. 

The actual path we've been on has been: deep learning, scaling, finetuning with RLHF, and now (just starting) reflective agents built on a GPT base. 

A reflective GPT-based agent is certainly capable of studying itself and coming up with ideas for improvement. So we're probably at the beginning of attempts at self-improvement, right now.
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: The MIRI 2000s paradigm for an AI capable of self-improvement, was that it would be modular code with a hierarchical organization, that would potentially engage in self-improvement at every level.

summary: The main claim is: I think those are canaries in the coal mine, and we will see more and more people forming emotional bonds with AIs.
One can argue that such AI might be good for lonely people or to be some sort of more affordable way of emotional and mental health support.
The main claim is: Write a Python script that uses the GPT Python API.
Locally, the Python script creates a fresh terminal session (say, bash) and says to GPT: Hi, GPT.
The main claim is: Was playing around with chat gpt and and some fun learning about its thoughts on metaphysics.
It looks like the ego is an illusion and hedonistic utilitarianism is too narrow minded to capture all of welfare.
The main claim is: However, the question is how many inferences we need to make per hour to simulate a human.
Estimates the cost of training AGI of that scale (tentative conclusion: $50B - $5T) 3.
The main claim is: The reason why misaligned ASI is a credible existential threat — a bar which few other technologies meet — is because of the "competent optimiser".
And if I can't fully persuade myself of the credible existential threat, then how am I to persuade others?
The main claim is: Scheutz (HRI Lab, Tufts University), Ethics and Information Technology, 2018.
Machines learning values, Steve Petersen (Niagara University), in Ethics of Artificial Intelligence, 2020.
